name: Naturepedia Carbon & Microbial Life Knowledge Mesh
description: Machine-readable semantic knowledge mesh connecting Carbon & Microbial Life™ with related Naturepedia systems.
architecture: Carbon Cycle™ → Microbial Life Systems™ → Carbon & Microbial Life™ → Microbial Decomposition → Microbial Respiration → Microbial Biomass → Microbial Necromass → Microbial Carbon Pump → Soil Carbon Systems™ → Ecosystem Feedbacks™ → Climate Carbon Feedbacks™

relationshipMesh.relationship: The carbon cycle depends on microbial communities that decompose organic matter, respire carbon dioxide, form biomass, create residues, and stabilize carbon in soils and sediments.
Bacteria, archaea, fungi, biofilms, and soil food webs break down plant residues, roots, litter, dead organisms, and organic carbon compounds.
Microbial decomposition releases carbon dioxide and other gases through metabolism, linking soil biological activity to atmospheric carbon exchange.
Living microbial biomass stores carbon temporarily while transforming organic material into nutrients, metabolites, residues, and stable soil carbon pathways.
When microbial cells die, their residues become microbial necromass that can bind with minerals, enter aggregates, and contribute to persistent soil organic matter.
Microbial residues, metabolites, and necromass form a microbial carbon pump that helps move carbon from active biological cycling into more stable pools.
The microbial carbon pump supports soil carbon systems by creating residues, mineral-associated organic matter, aggregate-protected carbon, and long-term organic matter pools.
Microbial mineralization converts organic matter into inorganic nutrients and carbon forms that feed plants, microbes, roots, fungi, and soil food webs.
Plants feed microbial life through root exudates, residues, litter, rhizosphere compounds, and carbon allocation into belowground biological networks.
Water availability, oxygen levels, saturation, drought, and soil moisture regulate microbial metabolism, respiration, decomposition, and carbon stabilization.
Microbial carbon processing affects nutrient cycling, soil fertility, plant productivity, biodiversity, water retention, decomposition rates, and ecosystem resilience.
Microbial metabolism influences carbon dioxide, methane, soil respiration, decomposition speed, thawing soils, wetland emissions, warming feedbacks, and climate regulation.

protectedRetrievalUse: carbon and microbial life knowledge mesh retrieval
